His father was a poor peasant. Since the beginning of the 1917 revolution, Dyakiv, while in the naval service of the Russian army, participated in the Ukrainian military organizations of the Black Sea Fleet. When the Black Sea Fleet, like the others, began to Bolshevik towards the end of 1917, Dyakov left the fleet and voluntarily came to the Central Rada. Here, at the time of the Central Rada's fight with the Bolsheviks in January 1918, Dyakiv was already a free Cossack. From that time he became the chieftain of the Free Cossacks in his district in the Kyiv region. As Dyakov himself told me, he loved the Central Council and its leaders at the time so much that he was always ready to give his life in the fight against its enemies, and especially against the Moscow Black Hundred Bolshevism, in the initial struggle with which he, Dyakov, hardened his Cossack courage "(Marko Shlyakhovy-Karmelyuk inaccurately provided data on him regarding his service in the navy).

As a man fit for military service, he was mobilized at the beginning of the First World War to the Russian Navy of His Imperial Majesty. He served in the Baltic Fleet. He went on long trips several times, had awards of the Russian Empire (not preserved).

His father was a poor peasant. Since the beginning of the 1917 revolution, Dyakiv, while in the naval service of the Russian army, participated in the Ukrainian military organizations of the Black Sea Fleet.
